message:
fix(signal): sigaltstack SS_DISABLE bypasses TLS-touching policy gate

Adds a fast path to sigaltstack() for the SS_DISABLE flag so that
cleanup calls coming out of Rust's runtime (thread-exit and
process-exit shutdown sequences) do not recurse back into the
runtime-policy gate or any TLS-backed helpers.

crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/signal_abi.rs:

- Before touching the runtime_policy::decide + MembraneAction path,
  check whether `ss` is non-null and (*ss).ss_flags has SS_DISABLE
  set. If yes, go straight to raw_syscall::sys_sigaltstack with
  the raw pointers, convert an errno result via set_abi_errno,
  and return.
- All other signed-in/out combinations (enable, allow-with-stack,
  query-only old_ss) still run through the normal policy decision
  path, so the hot path is strictly narrower than the general
  case.

Motivation: during thread-exit and process-exit, Rust's runtime
calls sigaltstack(SS_DISABLE) on the per-thread signal stack. If
that call touches TLS while TLS is being torn down, we risk a
stack-overflow or use-after-free at the exact moment the process
is trying to unwind. The SS_DISABLE path is a simple raw syscall
and has no reason to involve the membrane decision state, so
short-circuiting is both safer and a measurable latency win on
exit.
